source_paper_bibtex: @article{Berger_2025, title={Pandora’s Problem with Combinatorial Cost}, volume={50}, ISSN={1526-5471}, url={http://dx.doi.org/10.1287/moor.2023.0248}, DOI={10.1287/moor.2023.0248}, number={4}, journal={Mathematics of Operations Research}, publisher={Institute for Operations Research and the Management Sciences (INFORMS)}, author={Berger, Ben and Ezra, Tomer and Feldman, Michal and Fusco, Federico}, year={2025}, month=nov, pages={3161–3189} }

Section 6 (Conclusion and Future Directions), page 25.

For example, it is not clear whether there exists a poly-time algorithm that finds an optimal (or an approximately optimal) strategy under budget-additive costs.
title: Polynomial-time optimal or approximate strategies for Pandora problem with budget-additive costs
background_latex:
Pandora's problem has a ground set of boxes \([n]=\{1,\dots,n\}\). Each box \(i\) contains an independent nonnegative random value \(V_i\sim D_i\) with finite expectation. A (possibly adaptive) strategy \(\pi\) sequentially chooses unopened boxes to inspect; inspecting reveals the realized value and contributes to the total inspection cost. After any inspection the strategy may halt and receive reward equal to the maximum realized value among inspected boxes.

A combinatorial inspection cost function is \(c:2^{[n]}\to \mathbb{R}_{\ge 0}\), assumed normalized and monotone: \(c(\varnothing)=0\) and \(c(S)\le c(T)\) for \(S\subseteq T\). If the strategy inspects exactly the (random) set \(S(\pi)\subseteq [n]\), its expected utility is
\[
 u(\pi)=\mathbb{E}\Big[\max_{i\in S(\pi)} V_i\Big]-\mathbb{E}[c(S(\pi))].
\]

A cost function is budget-additive (a.k.a. capped-additive) if there exist nonnegative weights \((w_i)_{i\in [n]}\) and a budget \(B\ge 0\) such that for all \(S\subseteq [n]\),
\[
 c(S)=\min\Big\{B,\sum_{i\in S} w_i\Big\}.
\]
This class is monotone and submodular. The computational model considered in the paper represents \(c\) via oracle access (cost queries): given \(S\), a query returns \(c(S)\).
problem_definition_latex:
Design an algorithm that, given oracle access to a budget-additive cost function \(c(S)=\min\{B,\sum_{i\in S} w_i\}\) and explicit descriptions of independent value distributions \((D_i)_{i\in[n]}\), computes either:

1) an optimal Pandora strategy \(\pi^*\in\arg\max_{\pi} u(\pi)\), or

2) a strategy \(\pi\) with provable approximation guarantee for \(\max_{\pi} u(\pi)\),

in time polynomial in \(n\) (and the input encoding / desired accuracy), using only polynomially many cost queries.
latex:
Pandora's problem has a ground set of boxes \([n]=\{1,\dots,n\}\). Each box \(i\) contains an independent nonnegative random value \(V_i\sim D_i\) with finite expectation. A (possibly adaptive) strategy \(\pi\) sequentially chooses unopened boxes to inspect; inspecting reveals the realized value and contributes to the total inspection cost. After any inspection the strategy may halt and receive reward equal to the maximum realized value among inspected boxes.

A combinatorial inspection cost function is \(c:2^{[n]}\to \mathbb{R}_{\ge 0}\), assumed normalized and monotone: \(c(\varnothing)=0\) and \(c(S)\le c(T)\) for \(S\subseteq T\). If the strategy inspects exactly the (random) set \(S(\pi)\subseteq [n]\), its expected utility is
\[

\]

Design an algorithm that, given oracle access to a budget-additive cost function \(c(S)=\min\{B,\sum_{i\in S} w_i\}\) and explicit descriptions of independent value distributions \((D_i)_{i\in[n]}\), computes either:

1) an optimal Pandora strategy \(\pi^*\in\arg\max_{\pi} u(\pi)\), or

2) a strategy \(\pi\) with provable approximation guarantee for \(\max_{\pi} u(\pi)\),

open_problem_quote: For example, it is not clear whether there exists a poly-time algorithm that finds an optimal (or an approximately optimal) strategy under budget-additive costs.
counterexample_suitability_rationale: The statement is existential about algorithms (efficient optimal/approximate computation), not a crisp universal inequality that can be falsified by a single finite instance. While one can experiment on instances, failure of specific heuristics does not constitute a counterexample to existence of a polynomial-time algorithm.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: Candidate strategies/algorithms (e.g., parameterized fixed-order rules, dynamic programs, or approximation heuristics) can be encoded and evaluated on simulated instances to optimize empirical utility or worst-case observed ratios. However, the target is a worst-case polynomial-time guarantee, and empirical fitness signals are only partial proxies for proving approximation ratios or exact optimality.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.

literature_review.synthesis:
The source paper (Berger–Ezra–Feldman–Fusco, 2023) initiates Pandora’s problem with combinatorial inspection costs and proves a sharp separation between structure and computation. For all submodular cost functions, there exists an optimal strategy with a non-adaptive exploration order (a fixed permutation with stopping thresholds), extending Weitzman’s fixed-order phenomenon beyond additive costs. However, under oracle access to a general submodular cost function, even deciding whether any strategy achieves positive expected utility requires super-polynomially many cost queries (via indistinguishability constructions inspired by Svitkina–Fleischer), ruling out any polynomial-query approximation in that broad model.

Budget-additive costs, defined by \(c(S)=\min\{B,\sum_{i\in S} w_i\}\), sit inside submodular costs but have much more structure: they are determined by \((w_i)\) and \(B\), and their marginal costs are simple until the cap binds. The impossibility result for arbitrary submodular costs therefore does not directly resolve the budget-additive case, and the paper explicitly leaves open whether one can compute an optimal or approximately optimal strategy in polynomial time with only polynomially many cost queries. The classical additive-cost case (Weitzman) is efficiently solvable via reservation values, but Example 2 in the source paper shows that naive reservation-value reasoning can fail badly under non-additive submodular costs, suggesting that new algorithmic ideas are needed.

Related lines of work on Pandora variants (e.g., order constraints and nonobligatory inspection) show that fixed-order structure and efficient computation can diverge, and that PTAS techniques can sometimes be developed even when exact optimization is hard. For the budget-additive open problem, promising directions include exploiting the explicit parametric form of the cost (learning \(w_i\) and \(B\) from queries, or assuming they are given), reducing the search for an optimal fixed order to a tractable optimization over permutations and thresholds, and designing approximation schemes that discretize values and reason about when the cap \(B\) becomes binding.

sources.title: Optimal search for the best alternative
sources.theorem_or_result: Introduces Pandora’s problem with additive inspection costs and proves an optimal non-adaptive index (reservation value) policy: open boxes in decreasing reservation value and stop when the current best exceeds the next index.
sources.connection: Serves as the baseline: with additive costs (a special case of budget-additive when the cap never binds), the problem is solvable efficiently with a fixed order. The open problem asks whether similar polynomial-time solvability/approximability holds for budget-additive costs, which are submodular but non-additive due to the cap.

sources.title: Delegated search approximates efficient search
sources.theorem_or_result: Studies variants of Pandora-like search and shows approximation guarantees for delegated/strategic search settings, leveraging index-like structure in related models.
sources.connection: Provides conceptual and technical background on index/threshold policies and their robustness in extensions of Pandora’s problem, though it does not address combinatorial (budget-additive) inspection costs.

sources.title: Pandora’s box problem with order constraints
sources.theorem_or_result: Analyzes Pandora’s box under precedence constraints; shows fixed-order optimality and efficient computation for tree constraints, but NP-hardness and need for adaptivity under general precedence graphs.
sources.connection: The source paper uses this work to relate adaptivity phenomena beyond submodular costs and to motivate structural questions. It is indirectly relevant: budget-additive costs are submodular, so fixed-order optimality holds structurally, but computational tractability remains open.

sources.title: Submodular approximation: Sampling-based algorithms and lower bounds
sources.theorem_or_result: Develops query-complexity lower bounds for submodular optimization and constructs hard-to-distinguish submodular functions under value (oracle) queries.
sources.connection: The source paper’s computational impossibility for submodular-cost Pandora leverages the indistinguishability framework from this work. For budget-additive costs, which are a restricted submodular class, these lower bounds may not apply directly, motivating the open problem.

sources.title: The price of information in combinatorial optimization
sources.theorem_or_result: Introduces an adaptivity-gap framework for stochastic probing/selection problems, relating adaptive and non-adaptive policies and giving approximation algorithms in several models.
sources.connection: Budget-additive costs are submodular, and the source paper proves adaptivity is not needed structurally for all submodular costs. Singla’s techniques are relevant as potential tools for approximation under restricted cost classes like budget-additive, though not directly solving Pandora with capped-additive inspection costs.

sources.title: Pandora’s problem with nonobligatory inspection: Optimal structure and a PTAS
sources.theorem_or_result: Shows Pandora’s problem with nonobligatory inspection is NP-hard but admits a PTAS; also characterizes structural properties of near-optimal strategies.
sources.connection: Demonstrates that small rule changes can yield PTAS despite NP-hardness. While the cost model differs, PTAS design ideas (discretization, dynamic programming over thresholds) may inspire approximation approaches for budget-additive inspection costs.

sources.title: Whether or not to open Pandora’s box
sources.theorem_or_result: Introduces Pandora’s problem with non-obligatory inspection and shows optimal strategies may require adaptively choosing which boxes to inspect.
sources.connection: Highlights that adaptivity can become necessary under modified rules even with additive costs; contrasts with the submodular-cost setting where fixed-order optimality holds. Not directly about budget-additive costs but informs structural expectations.
